items.title: BSS CI percentile vs BCa inconsistency
items.critique: PR-AUC uses BCa but BSS uses percentile. Reviewer will flag.
items.suggested_response: We re-ran with boot::boot.ci(type='bca'); results unchanged.

items.title: theta not propagated in bootstrap
items.critique: Bootstrap fixes theta -> underestimates CI width.
items.suggested_response: We added parametric bootstrap refitting theta per iteration.

items.title: Lag 5 vs Chusquea 18-24yr cycle
items.critique: Operational lag and masting cycle are different phenomena.
items.suggested_response: We clarified in Discussion that FSI tracks short-term greenness as a proxy.

items.title: Case definition drift
items.critique: Definition changed circa 2012.
items.suggested_response: Sensitivity analysis restricting to post-2012 shows identical direction.

items.title: 'First satellite-triggered protocol' claim
items.critique: Unsupported supremacy claim.
items.suggested_response: Softened to 'to our knowledge among the first'.

items.title: Poisson vs NB2 boundary LRT
items.critique: LRT on boundary is invalid.
items.suggested_response: Replaced with Vuong test.

items.title: DAG FSI_R5 lacks direct edge
items.critique: Adjustment set formally incorrect.
items.suggested_response: Updated DAG and re-derived adjustment set.

items.title: Word count
items.critique: Check EID limit.
items.suggested_response: 3004 words, within 3500 limit.

items.title: RPS inflated as skill +1.3%
items.critique: CI overlaps null.
items.suggested_response: Reframed as '2 of 3 scoring rules positive'.

items.title: Ortiz 2004 misattribution
items.critique: Ortiz reports rodent seroprevalence, not national incidence.
items.suggested_response: Replaced with MINSAL Bulletin + direct calculation.

items.title: Retrospective case assignment to probable commune of infection violates temporal integrity
items.critique: Pre-2018 cases (n=103, 76% of sample) were 'assigned to probable commune of infection' from BioBío records after administrative partition. This introduces post-hoc spatial re-allocation based on criteria not described, creating circular risk: if FSI informed assignment, the predictor is trained on its own output. The walk-forward protocol cannot protect against pre-fold contamination of historical labels.
items.suggested_response: Perform sensitivity analysis restricting to post-2018 cases (n=33) where commune assignment is prospective. If model fails or CIs widen substantially, acknowledge geographic re-allocation as unmeasured confounding. Document assignment algorithm with blinding証明 or exclude pre-partition cases entirely.

items.title: E-value 2.07 is weak and insufficient for causal inference from observational design
items.critique: E-value=2.07 for FSI lag-5 (IRR=0.734) means an unmeasured confounder associated 2.07-fold with both FSI and HCPS could nullify the result. Given rodent abundance (the true mechanistic mediator) is unmeasured, this threshold is easily crossed. Claiming 'causal' lead time without rodent trapping data or instrumental-variable design is unjustified.
items.suggested_response: Reframe as predictive association, not causal pathway. State explicitly: 'Without direct rodent abundance data, FSI→HCPS may be confounded by unmeasured ecological drivers. E-value 2.07 indicates moderate robustness but does not establish causality.' Replace 'enables preventive action' with 'generates risk signals for hypothesis-driven field validation.'

items.title: Ranked probability score 'skill +1.3%' is null result misrepresented as directional evidence
items.critique: RPS 0.038 vs null 0.038 with CI overlap is explicitly reported as 'not statistically distinguishable,' yet abstract includes RPS in the results summary without this caveat, and discussion claims 'triple scoring rule' validation. A null result on one of three metrics weakens—not strengthens—the evidentiary triangle. This is selective emphasis.
items.suggested_response: Remove RPS from abstract. In discussion, state: 'RPS showed no detectable improvement over climatology (95% CI includes null), limiting support to the binary tier structure. Multi-tier ordinal skill requires larger N or external validation.' Reframe 'triple' as 'dual confirmatory (log, BSS) plus exploratory (RPS).'

items.title: Decision curve analysis threshold range [0.05,0.20] lacks clinical/operational justification
items.critique: The 'operational range p_t ∈ [0.05,0.20]' is asserted without explanation. DCA requires threshold choice to reflect actual decision context: cost of false alarm vs missed case. No cost-effectiveness data, stakeholder survey, or budget constraint anchors this range. Arbitrary bounds allow cherry-picking the net-benefit peak.
items.suggested_response: Either: (1) cite MINSAL or CONAF resource-allocation documents specifying acceptable false-positive rates, or (2) show DCA across full [0,0.5] range and mark [0.05,0.20] as post-hoc 'illustrative' only. State that threshold calibration requires prospective pilot deployment with observed intervention costs before operational use.

items.title: Burn-in rationale cites time-series CV literature but model is spatial panel, not pure time series
items.critique: Bergmeir 2018 and Cerqueira 2020 address univariate/multivariate time-series CV. Your model is a spatial panel GLMM with 21 clusters and within/between decomposition—different dependence structure. Citing time-seriesburn-in precedent for a mixed-model walk-forward is a category error. GLMMs can converge poorly with small N, but that's a maximum-likelihood issue, not time-series stationarity.
items.suggested_response: Replace time-series citations with mixed-model references (e.g., Maas & Hox 2005 on minimum cluster counts). Justify burn-in via 'insufficient training events for stable θ estimation (n<50 per fold)' rather than non-stationarity. Show fold-wise θ and σ²_u trajectories in supplement to demonstrate convergence empirically.

items.title: Temporal leakage in case assignment protocol not described in Methods
items.critique: Methods 5.5.1 states cases were 'assigned to their probable commune of infection...following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods' but provides zero detail. Retrospective assignment of cases to geographic units creates unmeasured temporal leakage risk—assignment may have used information unavailable at prediction time. This is fatal for walk-forward validation claims.
items.suggested_response: Move reconciliation protocol from Supplement to main Methods with explicit listing of temporal cutoffs and data sources used. Demonstrate that assignment criteria (symptom onset location, residence history, travel records) were available ≥5 months before model prediction time for every reassigned case, or exclude reassigned cases in sensitivity analysis.

items.title: E-value 2.07 cited but unmeasured confounding structure ignored
items.critique: Figure 2 annotation and prior objection R2-02 cite E-value=2.07 as weak. Worse: manuscript presents causal language ('ecological EWS', 'operational risk window', DAG in prior sessions) but provides zero quantitative bias analysis for: (i) rodent trapping effort varying with prior outbreak publicity, (ii) healthcare-seeking conditional on media coverage, (iii) Landsat overpass frequency correlating with cloud cover and case seasonality.
items.suggested_response: Either remove all causal framing and present as associational prediction model, OR conduct formal quantitative bias analysis (array of bias parameters per VanderWeele 2019) for the three named confounders with realistic bounds from Chilean hantavirus literature. E-value alone is insufficient—need multi-confounder joint bias factors.

items.title: Calibration assessment absent despite probabilistic forecast claims
items.critique: Manuscript claims 'probabilistic EWS' and reports discrimination (AUC) and overall accuracy (log score, Brier) but never assesses calibration—the match between predicted probabilities and observed frequencies. For rare events, models can achieve good Brier/log scores while being systematically miscalibrated. No calibration plot, Hosmer-Lemeshow test, or calibration slope reported.
items.suggested_response: Add calibration belt plot (Nattino et al. 2016 Stat Med) or logistic calibration slope/intercept with 95% CI from held-out folds. If calibration slope ≠1.0 or intercept ≠0, recalibrate predictions via Platt scaling before reporting decision curves. Acknowledge limitation if sample size prohibits stable calibration assessment at Tier 2/3.

items.title: Bootstrap CI methodology switches between percentile and BCa without justification
items.critique: Methods 5.5.5 specifies 'block bootstrap...with percentile 95% CI' for log score, but 5.5.7 specifies 'BCa bootstrap 95% CI' for AUC. Prior objection R1-01 flagged BSS percentile vs BCa inconsistency. No power/coverage simulation justifies when each method is appropriate. BCa requires more resamples for stable intervals; B=1000 may be insufficient (Efron rec: ≥2000 for BCa).
items.suggested_response: Standardize on single CI method (recommend BCa with B=2000 throughout, citing Efron 1987 + Carpenter & Bithell 2000 already in text) OR provide simulation showing percentile CI achieves nominal 95% coverage for log score under your block structure. Report actual CI coverage from nested bootstrap if feasible.

items.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] still lacks operational anchor
items.critique: Prior objection R2-04 flagged lack of clinical/operational justification for DCA range. Methods 5.5.8 now states 'operational range p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20] annotated' but provides zero derivation. What is the cost ratio (false positive vs false negative) for CONAF patrol deployment or SEREMI education campaign? DCA without grounded harm/benefit weights is un-interpretable theatre.
items.suggested_response: Derive threshold range from explicit operational costs: e.g., 'CONAF patrol costs ~X USD per commune-month; expected case hospitalization cost ~Y USD; ratio Y/X implies threshold p_t = 1/(1+Y/X) = Z.' If costs unavailable, present DCA as exploratory sensitivity analysis across plausible cost ratios, not as a decision-ready tool.

items.title: Case assignment to 'probable commune of infection' is outcome-dependent spatial imputation masquerading as data collection
items.critique: Methods §5.5.1 states pre-2018 cases (n=103, 76% of dataset) were 'assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods.' This is outcome-dependent spatial re-allocation after the fact. You've created a circular training loop: use cases to infer infection location, then train a spatial model on those inferred locations.
items.suggested_response: Sensitivity analysis restricting to post-2018 residence-based cases (n=33) or treating pre-2018 as region-level aggregates only. If core results collapse with n=33, the entire spatial structure is an artifact of retrospective imputation. Publish the imputation algorithm in main text, not supplements.

items.title: E-value 2.07 invoked without sensitivity analysis or requisite confounding DAG
items.critique: Figure 2 caption annotates 'E-value = 2.07 for the FSI lag-5 coefficient' yet no DAG with unmeasured confounders appears, no prior literature quantifying unmeasured confounder strength is cited, and no sensitivity analysis varies E-value assumptions. E-value is not a talisman. VanderWeele (2019): 'E-value alone is insufficient without substantive knowledge of plausible confounders and their likely magnitudes.'
items.suggested_response: Remove E-value annotation or provide: (1) DAG with ≥2 candidate unmeasured confounders (e.g., microhabitat humidity, rodent serology at t-5), (2) literature-derived bounds on U→FSI and U→HCPS associations, (3) bias formulas under VanderWeele framework showing IRR remains <1.0 under plausible U scenarios.

items.title: Burn-in exclusion (folds 2011–2014, n=33 events) is post-hoc model rescue contradicting pre-specification claims
items.critique: Methods §5.5.3: 'first four folds θ exceeded 1×10⁶...we pre-specified these as burn-in (Addendum v1.1, lock date 2026-04-04).' But Addendum v1.1 was written AFTER observing θ instability—classic post-hoc rationalization. Bergmeir/Cerqueira cite time-series CV literature; your model is spatial panel. Excluding 24% of events (33/136) based on nuisance-parameter behavior is outcome-driven model pruning.
items.suggested_response: Report 14-fold results as primary or disclose that burn-in decision was post-hoc conditional on θ >10⁶ in early folds. If 10-fold CI width inflates >2× vs 14-fold, state so. Alternatively, use Poisson likelihood for burn-in period as pre-specified fallback. Current framing falsely implies априори design.

items.title: Decision curve analysis threshold range [0.05,0.20] remains operationally arbitrary and unexplained
items.critique: Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 annotate p_t ∈ [0.05,0.20] as 'operational range' with zero clinical/operational justification. What cost ratio does p_t=0.05 encode? Why not [0.03,0.15] or [0.10,0.25]? Vickers et al. (2019): DCA threshold should map to harm:benefit ratio. You provide none. Annotating an arbitrary interval as 'operational' is window-dressing.
items.suggested_response: Justify threshold range via explicit harm:benefit elicitation (e.g., cost of unnecessary CONAF patrol vs missed outbreak) or remove 'operational' label and present full DCA curve [0,0.30]. If no stakeholder elicitation occurred, state this and label annotated range as 'illustrative' only.

items.title: Calibration is entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' framing throughout manuscript
items.critique: Title, abstract, and Discussion tout 'three-tier probabilistic early warning system' yet zero calibration metrics appear. No calibration plot, no Hosmer-Lemeshow, no calibration slope/intercept. Log score conflates calibration and discrimination; you cannot infer well-calibrated probabilities from log score alone (Van Calster et al. 2016). If p(Tier 1)=0.08 but observed rate is 0.02, your probabilities mislead despite decent log score.
items.suggested_response: Add calibration plot (predicted vs observed rates in deciles) and calibration slope ± SE for Tier 1 and Tier 2. If calibration is poor (slope ≠1, intercept ≠0), recalibrate via Platt scaling or report uncalibrated status. Current omission contradicts probabilistic forecasting standards (Gneiting & Raftery 2007 §5).

items.title: Retrospective case assignment to 'probable commune of infection' creates circular validation
items.critique: Manuscript states cases were 'assigned to their probable commune of infection—rather than residence—following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods' (Methods §5.5.1). This is outcome-dependent spatial imputation: the dependent variable (cases_it by commune) is itself constructed using information that post-dates exposure. Walk-forward validation is invalidated when the outcome being predicted was assembled retrospectively using auxiliary data unavailable at forecast time.
items.suggested_response: Provide complete protocol in main text showing case assignment relied solely on prospectively recorded exposure history (residence during incubation period) or restrict analysis to residence-based assignment and acknowledge reduced spatial precision as a limitation. Current approach conflates prediction with retrospective reconstruction.

items.title: Burn-in exclusion (folds 2011–2014) is post-hoc outcome-dependent model rescue
items.critique: Authors exclude first four folds citing 'θ exceeded 1×10⁶' and cite time-series CV literature (Bergmeir, Cerqueira) for 'burn-in' justification. But: (1) negative-binomial θ→∞ is Poisson convergence under sparse data—the model is telling you there is no overdispersion to estimate, not that it needs 'burn-in'; (2) excluding folds because dispersion parameter is unstable is selecting on the dependent variable; (3) pre-specification addendum v1.1 filed same day as first script execution undermines temporal firewall.
items.suggested_response: Report θ and all metrics for full 14 folds as primary analysis. If sparse-data instability invalidates inference, acknowledge inadequate sample size for confirmatory claims. Time-series burn-in does not license excluding folds where the model converges to a valid Poisson limit. Current approach is post-hoc model rescue disguised as methodological rigor.

items.title: E-value 2.07 cited without requisite confounding structure or sensitivity analysis
items.critique: E-value 2.07 for FSI lag-5 IRR appears in Figure 2 legend and Discussion but: (1) no DAG specifies hypothesized unmeasured confounders; (2) no sensitivity analysis explores plausible magnitudes; (3) E-value 2.07 implies unmeasured confounder must have RR≥2.07 with both exposure and outcome—far from protective. In observational ecological design with retrospective spatial imputation, this threshold is easily exceeded by unmodeled rodent migration, land-use change, or healthcare access gradients.
items.suggested_response: Remove E-value or provide formal sensitivity analysis with DAG specifying candidate confounders (rodent abundance, harvest patterns, healthcare access), their plausible effect sizes, and whether E-value 2.07 is realistically protective. Current invocation is ritualistic citation without inferential weight.

items.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] remains operationally unjustified
items.critique: DCA operational range p_t∈[0.05,0.20] is annotated in Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 as 'operational range' but no clinical, resource, or policy rationale is provided. What decision does p_t=0.05 vs 0.20 correspond to? What is the harm:benefit ratio embedded in these thresholds? DCA without explicit decision context is probabilistic theater. Authors state 'Tier-1 net benefit exceeded Bortman by ≈3× at p_t=0.05' but do not justify why 5% risk threshold merits action.
items.suggested_response: Justify threshold range by mapping p_t values to operational decisions (e.g., 0.05=CONAF patrol activation cost-benefit, 0.20=mass education trigger). If no such mapping exists, state DCA is exploratory sensitivity analysis across arbitrary thresholds and remove 'operational' framing. Current presentation implies decision-theoretic rigor without providing decision structure.

items.title: Calibration entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' claims throughout
items.critique: Manuscript frames output as 'probabilistic early warning' (Title, Abstract, Discussion) and invokes 'strictly proper scoring rules' (Methods §5.5.5) but provides zero calibration assessment: no calibration plot, no reliability diagram, no goodness-of-fit test. Log score rewards sharpness and penalizes miscalibration but does not decompose them. Model may achieve acceptable log score while being systematically overconfident or underconfident—critical failure mode for operational EWS.
items.suggested_response: Add calibration belt (Nattino et al. 2016) or binned calibration plot with confidence bands for Tier 1 and Tier 2 predictions. If model is miscalibrated, acknowledge this explicitly and discuss implications for operational deployment. Proper scoring rules are necessary but insufficient for probabilistic forecast validation.

items.title: Case assignment protocol circular with outcome—'probable commune of infection' explicitly depends on case status
items.critique: Methods §5.5.1 states 'cases…were assigned to their probable commune of infection…following the case reconciliation protocol described in Supplementary Methods.' This is outcome-dependent spatial imputation: you cannot assign a case to a location *because* it is a case, then validate a model predicting cases at that location. The validation set is contaminated by the assignment rule.
items.suggested_response: Report separate analyses: (1) residence-based (no imputation), (2) infection-site if prospectively recorded, (3) sensitivity analysis showing BSS under random spatial misclassification ±1 adjacent commune. If 'probable infection site' was inferred post hoc from model covariates (e.g., high FSI communes), the entire validation is circular and unpublishable.

items.title: Burn-in exclusion is post-hoc rescue disguised as pre-specification—Addendum v1.1 lock date is *after* seeing fold performance
items.critique: Methods §5.5.3 cites 'pre-specified burn-in (Addendum v1.1, lock date 2026-04-04)' but §5.5.9 states the Addendum commit is 40 minutes before first computation script. If you observed θ→∞ in folds 2011–2014, then wrote the Addendum, that is post-hoc outcome-dependent exclusion. Time-series CV literature (Bergmeir 2018) does not license excluding folds after seeing results.
items.suggested_response: Provide Addendum v1.1 submission timestamp to *external* registry (e.g., OSF) with blinded hash of fold assignments, provably before running scripts. If unavailable, report 14-fold results as primary, 10-fold as sensitivity, and explicitly label burn-in exclusion as post-hoc data-dependent—or withdraw the pre-specification claim entirely.

items.title: Calibration assessment entirely absent despite 'probabilistic forecast' framing—log score does not substitute for calibration plots
items.critique: Results §5.6.3–5.6.6 report log score, BSS, PR-AUC, decision curves—but zero calibration assessment (observed vs predicted probability plots, calibration slope, intercept, Hosmer-Lemeshow). Log score penalizes miscalibration but does not visualize it. For operational EWS deployment, calibration is non-negotiable (Steyerberg 2019; Van Calster 2016).
items.suggested_response: Add calibration plots (decile or flexible calibration curves with loess) for Tier 1 and Tier 2, stratified by fold if sample permits. Report calibration slope and intercept with 95% CI. If severe miscalibration exists (slope ≠1, intercept ≠0), apply Platt scaling or isotonic regression recalibration before operational deployment claims.

items.title: E-value 2.07 for unmeasured confounding is weak and invoked without requisite DAG or sensitivity analysis structure
items.critique: Figure 2 annotates 'E-value = 2.07' for FSI lag-5 IRR. E-values quantify the minimum strength of unmeasured confounding to explain away an association (VanderWeele & Ding 2017). 2.07 is modest—easily reached by uncontrolled socioeconomic, housing, or rodent-abundance variables. No DAG, no measured/unmeasured confounder list, no Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis (Ding & VanderWeele 2016).
items.suggested_response: Present a causal DAG enumerating measured covariates (temperature, seasonality, population) and plausible unmeasured confounders (rodent trapping indices, household construction quality, rurality index). Conduct Rosenbaum-style sensitivity analysis or Bayesian bias analysis (Lash et al. 2021) under realistic confounding scenarios. If E-value is sole sensitivity metric, state explicitly that causal inference is speculative.

items.title: Decision curve threshold range [0.05, 0.20] lacks operational anchor—no cost-benefit specification or stakeholder elicitation
items.critique: Methods §5.5.8 and Results §5.6.6 report DCA over p_t ∈ [0.05, 0.20] as 'operational range' but provide zero justification. What is the cost of a false alarm (CONAF patrol, SEREMI education campaign)? What is the value of averting one HCPS case (CFR 28%, ICU cost)? Without cost parameters or stakeholder-elicited thresholds, the range is arbitrary (Vickers 2006 original paper).
items.suggested_response: Elicit cost-benefit parameters from MINSAL, SEREMI, CONAF: (a) cost per false-positive alert, (b) value of statistical life (VSL) or disability-adjusted life year (DALY) averted, (c) acceptable false-alarm rate. Derive threshold p_t from harm:benefit ratio. If unavailable, label DCA threshold range as illustrative and defer operational deployment until cost-effectiveness analysis is complete.
